merger_description:
WPF Duratec Pty Ltd (WPF Duratec), a wholly owned subsidiary of Australian engineering, construction and remediation contractor Duratec Limited (Duratec) (ASX:DUR), proposes to acquire 100% of the shares in Davhold Australia Pty Ltd, trading as Pacific Welding Australia.

Duratec is an Australian engineering, construction and remediation business. WPF Duratec is a specialist engineering, fabrication and site services provider based in Western Australia.

Pacific Welding Australia is an Australian welding and fabrication company based in Newcastle, New South Wales. Pacific Welding Australia specialises in the provision of project based welding, mechanical services and specialist fabrication to clients in the oil & gas, energy and mining industries.

events.title: WPF Duratec - Pacific Welding - Phase 1 determination
events.determination_commission_division: Determination made by Commissioner Williams pursuant to a delegation under section 25(1) of the Act

determination_table_content.details:
WPF Duratec Pty Ltd (WPF Duratec) proposes to acquire 100% of
the shares in Davhold Australia Pty Ltd, trading as Pacific Welding
Australia (Pacific Welding) (the Acquisition).

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission has
determined under section 51ABZE(1) of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) that the Acquisition may be put into effect.

The acquirer, WPF Duratec, is a wholly owned subsidiary of
Australian engineering, construction and remediation contractor
Duratec Limited (Duratec) (ASX:DUR). WPF Duratec is a specialist
engineering, fabrication and site services provider based in Western
Australia.
The target, Pacific Welding, is an Australian welding and fabrication
company based in Newcastle, New South Wales. Pacific Welding
specialises in the provision of project based welding, mechanical
services and specialist fabrication to clients in the oil & gas, energy
and mining industries.

determination_table_content.item:
Overlap and relationship
between the parties
determination_table_content.details:
WPF Duratec and Pacific Welding (the Parties) overlap in the supply
of the following project-based welding, mechanical and labour hire
services (the Services):
• structural, mechanical and piping fabrication and installation
• pipelines and facilities construction
• vessel fabrication
• industrial asset maintenance services
• specialised welding services
• shutdown / outage / turnaround management.
There is limited geographic overlap between the Parties in the supply
of the Services as they operate in different states or territories (WPF
Duratec in Western Australia and the Northern Territory, and Pacific
Welding in New South Wales).
There is no existing vertical or conglomerate relationship between
the Parties, but there may be in the future absent the Acquisition as
subcontracting arrangements are common in the industry.

When making a determination in Phase 1, the Australian Competition
and Consumer Commission (ACCC) undertakes a competition
assessment and considers whether it is appropriate for an
acquisition to be approved or subject to further assessment in
Phase 2 in accordance with section 51ABZJ of the Competition and
Consumer Act 2010 (Cth) (the Act). In doing so, the ACCC must have
regard to the object of the Act and all relevant matters, including the
interests of consumers.
For more information about the ACCC’s approach to considering
notified acquisitions, see the ACCC’s merger assessment guidelines
and interim merger process guidelines.
In conducting its competition assessment, the ACCC has considered
the information and documents that were submitted with the
notification form, in response to a request for information,
information from third parties and publicly available information.
The ACCC has determined that the Acquisition may be put into
effect as it considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to have the
effect of substantially lessening competition in any market. In
reaching its decision, and based on the material before it, the ACCC
makes the following findings:
• There is limited geographic overlap between WPF Duratec and
Pacific Welding as they supply these services in different states
and territories. Therefore, the ACCC considers that the Parties
are not close competitors and likely compete for different
customers.
• The Parties would likely continue to be competitively constrained
by a range of alternative suppliers of the Services, including
AllType Engineering and Piping Solutions.
• The ACCC considers that the Acquisition is unlikely to give WPF
Duratec the ability or incentive to foreclose rivals accessing the
Services offered by Pacific Welding due to the existence of the
competitors identified above and the limited geographic overlap
between the Parties.
The ACCC received divergent views regarding competition from
other suppliers of the Services (beyond those identified above),
including Monadelphous, UGL, Civmec and M&S Fabrication, and the
extent to which these companies provide the full range of Services
offered by the Parties. The ACCC has taken this feedback into
account in its competition assessment.
The ACCC also received concerns from some market participants
regarding consolidation within the industry more generally. However,
these concerns were not sufficiently connected to a competition
issue arising from the Acquisition.

A notifying party, or other person who has been allowed to do so by
the Australian Competition Tribunal, may apply for review if they are
dissatisfied with the determination. Pursuant to section 100C of the
Act, applications for review of the determination are to be made to
the Australian Competition Tribunal before the end of 14 calendar
days after this statement of reasons was included on the ACCC’s
Acquisitions Register. To confirm whether there has been any
application for review, please contact the Australian Competition
Tribunal.
